title: Effective long-range attraction of moiré excitons under the influence of atomic reconstructions and anisotropic screening
authors: Nils-Erik Schütte, Carl Emil Mørch Nielsen, Niclas Götting, Alexander Steinhoff, Gabriel Bester, Christopher Gies
abstract: The moiré pattern, which emerges due to a relative rotation between two monolayers of transition metal dichalcogenides, features a long lattice period for small twist angles. The resulting band structure modulation acts as an effective potential for interlayer excitons (IXs), which can realize correlated many-body phenomena. Here, we aim for a material-realistic modelling of the exciton-exciton interaction, taking into account lattice reconstructions and an exciton-exciton potential that incorporates the highly anisotropic screening imposed by the two-dimensional bilayer and the dielectric background. We find strong modifications of the on-site interaction induced by the change of the moiré potential during lattice reconstructions, while for long-range interactions on the length scale of the moiré period, anisotropic dielectric screening leads to a crossover from a repulsive to an attractive interaction. The interaction potential and hopping amplitudes serve as parameters for a Bose-Hubbard model on the moiré lattice, which we use to explain correlated behavior of interlayer excitons.
summary: This paper investigates how the physical structure and surrounding environment of moire TMD bilayers change the way interlayer excitons interact. It reveals that anisotropic screening can turn repulsive interactions into attractive ones, potentially explaining observed many-body phenomena in these materials.
main_problem: The study aims to provide a material-realistic description of exciton-exciton interactions in TMD moire heterobilayers by accounting for both atomic reconstructions and highly anisotropic dielectric screening.
main_result: The researchers found that anisotropic screening causes a crossover from repulsive to attractive long-range interactions, and that atomic reconstructions significantly modify the on-site interaction strength.
method: The work uses classical force-field relaxations for atomic reconstruction, solves Poisson's equation for anisotropic dielectric environments, and employs an extended Bose-Hubbard model within a mean-field approximation.
model_or_system: The system consists of MoS2/WS2 transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) heterobilayers at small twist angles. The physics is modeled using an extended Bose-Hubbard model describing interlayer excitons as spinless bosons on a moire lattice.
key_observables: On-site interaction (U), nearest-neighbor interaction (V), hopping amplitude (t), and the moire potential depth.
important_parameters: Twist angles (1.0 to 3.9 degrees), dielectric environment (vacuum vs. hBN), and filling factor (rho).
main_assumptions: Excitons are treated as bosons neglecting internal fermionic structure; the variation of exciton binding energy across the lattice is neglected; and the K-K band gap is assumed to be direct.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares moire potential for different twist angles and rigid vs. reconstructed models; Figure 7 shows the crossover from repulsive to attractive interaction in different dielectric environments; Figure 8 shows Hubbard parameters as a function of twist angle; Figure 11 displays the Bose-Hubbard phase diagram.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of exciton interactions, details the modeling of atomic reconstruction and anisotropic screening, presents the derivation of the interaction potential, applies these to a Bose-Hubbard model to predict quantum phases, and concludes with experimental implications.
why_interesting: The discovery of an effective long-range attraction mediated by dielectric screening provides a new mechanism for controlling many-body phases like superfluidity and density waves in moire lattices, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
